---
title: "Theorem X.Y — <short name>"
lean_name: "<HansenEconometrics.fully.qualified.Name>"
chapter: N
---

## Textbook statement

For the relevant objects and hypotheses, Hansen's statement says:

$$\text{...}$$

### Proof sketch

Short mathematical proof idea. Keep this at the level of the textbook
argument; the Lean-specific mechanics belong in the hover walkthrough below.

## Lean statement

The corresponding Lean theorem:

- [`<name>`](<github source link>) at `HansenEconometrics/...lean:<line>`

::: {.lean-hover-panel}
```{=html}
<pre><code><span class="lean-token" tabindex="0" data-explain="The public theorem name; this should be citeable and stable.">theorem theorem_name</span>
    <span class="lean-token" tabindex="0" data-explain="Explicit mathematical inputs. Prefer these over hiding important assumptions inside opaque aliases.">(X : Matrix n k ℝ)</span>
    <span class="lean-token" tabindex="0" data-explain="Substantive assumptions should be named or packaged so downstream users can see what is being used.">(h : AssumptionPackage X)</span> :
    <span class="lean-token" tabindex="0" data-explain="The conclusion should match the chapter-facing mathematical statement, with bridge wrappers when needed.">Conclusion X</span> := by
  ...
</code></pre>
```
:::

### Reading the statement

Component-by-component interpretation table.

## Translation notes

Where the Lean phrasing differs from Hansen and why the difference is harmless.

## Tactic-by-tactic walkthrough

Use hoverable Lean lines for short proof bodies, or a compact proof map for
large composed theorems. Link supporting lemmas where they do real work.

## Downstream uses

- (optional) where this theorem is consumed elsewhere in the formalization.
